define.what: Main controller (PLC/SCADA) experiences software fault, communication loss, or processing error causing turbine shutdown
define.when: Can occur anytime, but 68% occur during grid instability events
define.impact: Complete turbine shutdown until controller is reset or repaired. Average 11 hours of lost production per episode.

measure.target: Less than 5 faults per turbine per year

analyze.root_cause: Controller software instability during grid frequency and voltage fluctuations
contributing: Grid instability triggers controller protection mode
Long alarm durations suggest manual intervention required (not auto-reset)
Affects all turbines equally — systemic issue, not turbine-specific

solutions: Update controller firmware to latest stable version with better grid fault tolerance
Implement auto-reset logic after fault diagnosis to reduce duration from 11 hrs to 2 hrs
Install redundant controller for critical turbines to eliminate 90% of downtime
Deploy grid stability monitoring for early warning before controller fault occurs
improve.expected_benefit: Software update reduces faults by 40%, auto-reset saves ~7,200 hours downtime. Combined LPF reduction: 3-4%

control.monitoring: Daily controller fault log review, weekly frequency trend analysis, monthly comparison to target
alerts: CRITICAL: More than 2 controller faults in 24 hours — immediate investigation
HIGH: More than 10 faults in 1 month — schedule controller inspection
MEDIUM: Fault duration exceeds 24 hours — escalate to vendor
control.review: Weekly team meeting to discuss controller health, monthly report to management

define.what: Prolonged loss of grid connection (more than 5 hours) preventing turbine from exporting power
define.when: Typically during severe weather, grid maintenance, or grid infrastructure failures
define.impact: Turbines cannot produce power even when wind is available. Total production loss during outage.

measure.target: Less than 5 outages per turbine per year with average duration under 3 hours

analyze.root_cause: Regional grid instability from aging grid infrastructure and weather events
contributing: Long durations suggest external grid issues beyond wind farm control
Affects all turbines simultaneously — grid-side problem, not turbine-side
Frequency increasing slightly over 9.9 year period suggesting grid aging

solutions: Establish direct communication protocol with grid operator for advance notice of planned outages
Install Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) to ride through short outages
Negotiate with grid operator for redundant connection point to reduce outage frequency by 80%
Use weather forecasting to predict high-risk outage periods and schedule maintenance accordingly
improve.expected_benefit: Communication protocol reduces surprise outages by 30%, saves ~3,200 hours. Combined LPF reduction: 2-3%

control.monitoring: Real-time grid voltage and frequency monitoring dashboard, daily review of outage logs
alerts: CRITICAL: Grid outage exceeds 2 hours — contact grid operator immediately
HIGH: More than 3 outages in 1 week — schedule meeting with grid operator
MEDIUM: Outage forecast based on weather — prepare emergency response team
control.review: Weekly internal team review, monthly meeting with grid operator, quarterly infrastructure upgrade business case

define.what: Grid frequency deviates outside acceptable range (typically 50 Hz plus or minus 0.5 Hz), triggering turbine safety shutdown
define.when: Occurs frequently (3,238 times over 9.9 years) but with short duration (37 min average)
define.impact: Turbine disconnects from grid until frequency stabilizes, then auto-reconnects. Brief but very frequent production loss.

measure.frequency: 3,238 episodes over 9.9 years (327/year, highest frequency alarm)
measure.target: Less than 20 events per turbine per year with auto-reconnect under 5 minutes

analyze.root_cause: Regional grid has poor frequency regulation due to high renewable penetration without adequate frequency control
contributing: Very high frequency (3,238 events) suggests chronic grid instability
Short duration (37 min) indicates grid recovers on its own
Auto-reconnect appears to work — not requiring manual intervention

solutions: Adjust frequency protection settings — widen acceptable band to plus or minus 0.6 Hz after confirming with grid code
Implement faster auto-reconnect logic to reduce reconnection delay from 37 min to under 5 min
Configure turbines to provide frequency support (synthetic inertia) to stabilize grid
Share data with grid operator showing frequency deviation impacts and request grid improvements
improve.expected_benefit: Adjusted settings reduce events by 40%, faster reconnect saves ~1,720 hours. Combined LPF reduction: 1-1.5%

control.monitoring: Real-time grid frequency monitoring with 1-second resolution, hourly deviation event log
alerts: CRITICAL: More than 10 frequency events in 24 hours — investigate grid instability
HIGH: Frequency event duration exceeds 2 hours — check auto-reconnect logic
MEDIUM: Increasing trend over 1 month — schedule grid operator meeting
control.review: Daily frequency stability check, weekly trend analysis, monthly report to grid operator

define.what: Brief complete loss of grid connection (under 2 hours), often caused by grid switching, faults cleared by auto-reclosers, or voltage sags
define.when: Very frequent (3,229 times over 9.9 years) with short duration (37 min average)
define.impact: Turbine disconnects, waits for grid stability, then auto-reconnects. Short but frequent production loss.

measure.target: Less than 15 events per turbine per year with reconnection under 3 minutes

analyze.root_cause: Grid auto-recloser operations and voltage sags from distant grid faults
contributing: Very high frequency suggests weak or fault-prone grid in region
Duration (37 min) is turbine reconnection delay, not grid recovery time
Pattern matches Grid Frequency Deviation — same reconnect logic applies

solutions: Implement Fast Frequency Response reconnection to reduce turbine reconnection delay from 37 min to under 3 min
Upgrade turbines to stay connected during voltage sags (Voltage Ride-Through capability)
Install better lightning arrestors on transmission lines to reduce lightning-triggered events by 30%
Request grid fault logs from operator to correlate with turbine trips and identify root causes
improve.expected_benefit: Faster reconnection saves ~1,830 hours, voltage ride-through reduces events by 50%. Combined LPF reduction: 1.5-2%

control.monitoring: Real-time 3-phase voltage monitoring, event logging capturing exact grid conditions at each trip
alerts: CRITICAL: More than 15 grid loss events in 24 hours — emergency grid operator contact
HIGH: Event duration exceeds 3 hours — investigate why auto-reconnect failed
MEDIUM: Clustering of events (more than 5 in 1 hour) — check for grid fault sequence
control.review: Daily event log review, weekly trend analysis and weather correlation, monthly joint review with grid operator

define.what: Grid voltage deviates outside acceptable operating range (typically plus or minus 10% of nominal), triggering turbine safety disconnect
define.when: Very frequent (3,181 times over 9.9 years), short duration (37 min average)
define.impact: Turbine protection system disconnects from grid until voltage stabilizes, then auto-reconnects.

measure.target: Less than 10 events per turbine per year with under 2 min reconnection

analyze.root_cause: Grid voltage regulation issues from weak grid infrastructure and large load switching
contributing: Forms a triplet with Grid Frequency Deviation and Momentary Grid Loss — often the same underlying event
Short duration indicates turbine reconnect delay, not grid recovery time
All turbines affected simultaneously — grid-side voltage problem

solutions: Widen voltage tolerance band to plus or minus 12% after checking grid code compliance
Configure turbines to provide reactive power support to stabilize grid voltage
Optimize coordinated reconnection logic — all three grid alarms share same 37-min timer, reduce to under 2 min
Work with grid operator to install voltage regulation equipment at point of interconnection
improve.expected_benefit: Wider tolerance reduces events by 35%, faster reconnect saves ~1,850 hours. Combined LPF reduction: 1.5-2%

control.monitoring: 3-phase voltage monitoring with 1-second resolution, daily voltage stability review
alerts: CRITICAL: More than 10 voltage events in 24 hours — emergency investigation
HIGH: Voltage event duration exceeds 3 hours — check reconnection logic failure
MEDIUM: Increasing events trend over 2 weeks — schedule grid operator meeting
control.review: Daily voltage event review, weekly detailed analysis, monthly joint meeting with grid operator

define.what: Mechanical emergency brake system activates to stop turbine rotor immediately
define.when: 1,263 times over 9.9 years, often triggered by overspeed conditions or grid loss events
define.impact: Rotor stops within seconds. Requires manual inspection before restart. Average 3.1 hours downtime per event.

measure.target: Less than 5 brake activations per turbine per year with under 1 hour downtime

analyze.root_cause: Triggered by overspeed protection when wind gusts exceed rotor speed limits, often following grid loss events
contributing: Higher than expected frequency suggests frequent overspeed conditions
3.1 hour average duration indicates manual inspection and reset required
Often occurs with grid loss alarms — grid trips cause sudden load loss then overspeed then brake

solutions: Optimize pitch controller to respond faster to wind gusts, preventing overspeed — reduces brake activations by 40%
Lower rotor speed setpoint slightly to increase margin before overspeed trigger
Implement automated brake diagnostic check and auto-restart if safe — reduces avg downtime from 3.1 hrs to 1 hr
Use LIDAR or upstream wind sensors to predict gusts and pitch blades preemptively
improve.expected_benefit: Pitch tuning reduces frequency by 40%, auto-reset saves ~2,650 hours downtime. Combined LPF reduction: 1.5-2%

control.monitoring: Real-time rotor speed monitoring with overspeed threshold alerts, daily brake event review
alerts: CRITICAL: Emergency brake fails to stop rotor — immediate turbine lockout
HIGH: More than 3 brake events on same turbine in 1 week — inspect pitch system
MEDIUM: Brake event during low wind — investigate spurious activation
control.review: Daily emergency brake event review, weekly pitch system performance analysis, monthly brake system inspection

define.what: General safety system triggers turbine shutdown from multiple possible safety sensors including vibration, temperature, oil pressure
define.when: 1,237 times over 9.9 years, similar frequency and duration to Emergency Brake Activation
define.impact: Immediate turbine shutdown, requires manual safety inspection before restart. Average 3.2 hours per event.

measure.target: Less than 8 safety shutdowns per turbine per year with under 1.5 hour downtime

analyze.root_cause: Multiple safety sensors triggering shutdowns — vibration, temperature, oil pressure, or emergency stop
contributing: Very similar frequency and duration to Emergency Brake Activation — likely related cascading events
3.2 hour duration indicates standardized safety inspection protocol required
Affects all turbines equally — systemic safety system settings or site conditions

solutions: Install data logging to identify which specific safety sensor is triggering most frequently
Install advanced vibration monitoring to detect issues before safety shutdown occurs
Optimize post-shutdown inspection process using remote diagnostics to reduce duration from 3.2 hrs to 1.5 hrs
Add more temperature sensors for early warning before safety limits are reached
improve.expected_benefit: Vibration monitoring reduces frequency by 35%, streamlined inspection saves ~2,103 hours. Combined LPF reduction: 1.5-2%

control.monitoring: Real-time all safety sensor monitoring dashboard, daily safety system activation review
alerts: CRITICAL: Safety system fails to trigger during unsafe condition — immediate investigation
HIGH: Same turbine has more than 4 safety shutdowns in 1 month — deep diagnostic required
MEDIUM: New pattern of safety activations — investigate environmental changes
control.review: Daily safety event log review, weekly pattern analysis, monthly safety system calibration check

define.what: Rotor speed exceeds maximum safe operating limit, triggering protective shutdown before overspeed damage occurs
define.when: 1,236 times over 9.9 years, nearly identical frequency to Emergency Brake and Safety System alarms
define.impact: Immediate pitch to feather and emergency brake activation. Manual restart required. Average 3.1 hours per event.

measure.target: Less than 3 overspeed events per turbine per year with under 1 hour downtime

analyze.root_cause: Wind gusts exceed pitch system response speed, or grid loss causes sudden load loss and rotor acceleration
contributing: Nearly identical frequency to Emergency Brake (1,263) and Safety System (1,237) — likely same events counted three ways
All turbines affected equally — site wind conditions or control settings
Likely triggered by grid loss events causing sudden load loss

solutions: Upgrade to faster pitch actuators and predictive pitch control — reduces overspeed events by 50%
During grid loss, simulate electrical load with emergency resistors to prevent rotor acceleration
Slightly increase overspeed threshold after aerodynamic analysis confirms safety
Implement LIDAR-based wind gust prediction for proactive pitch adjustments
improve.expected_benefit: Advanced pitch reduces frequency by 50%, grid loss load emulation prevents 60% of grid-loss overspeed events. Combined LPF reduction: 1.5-2%

control.monitoring: Real-time rotor speed monitoring with early warning at 90% of overspeed limit
alerts: CRITICAL: Overspeed protection fails — immediate turbine lockout
HIGH: More than 5 overspeed events on one turbine in 1 month — pitch system inspection
MEDIUM: Increasing trend of overspeed events — review control settings
control.review: Daily overspeed event log review, weekly rotor speed trend analysis, quarterly overspeed protection system test

define.what: Hydraulic system controlling nacelle yaw (directional positioning) experiences fault — low pressure, valve failure, or hydraulic motor malfunction
define.when: Only 19 times over 9.9 years (rare) but extremely long duration (68.5 hours average — nearly 3 days)
define.impact: Nacelle cannot yaw to optimal wind direction. Very long downtime due to complex repair requiring specialist access.

measure.frequency: 19 episodes over 9.9 years (2/year across all turbines)
measure.target: Less than 0.2 faults per turbine per year with under 24 hour downtime

analyze.root_cause: Hydraulic oil contamination causing valve sticking or motor damage, or hydraulic pump wear requiring replacement
contributing: Extremely long duration (68 hours) suggests parts must be ordered and specialist access required
Low frequency suggests component wear-out not design flaw
Random distribution across turbines — not site-specific issue

solutions: Stock critical yaw hydraulic components (pump, motor, valves) to reduce downtime from 68 hrs to 24 hrs
Install hydraulic pressure and temperature sensors with trend analysis for predictive maintenance
Implement regular hydraulic oil sampling and laboratory analysis quarterly
Pre-arrange rapid response OEM service contract to reduce response time by 24 hours
improve.expected_benefit: Spare parts reduce duration from 68 hrs to 24 hrs, condition monitoring prevents 50% of failures. Combined LPF reduction: 0.5-0.8%

control.monitoring: Daily yaw hydraulic pressure monitoring, weekly yaw system performance check
alerts: CRITICAL: Yaw hydraulic pressure below 50% of normal — immediate inspection
HIGH: Yaw system response time degrading — schedule maintenance
MEDIUM: Oil analysis shows contamination — plan oil change
control.review: Weekly yaw system health check, monthly hydraulic system trend review, quarterly yaw component wear inspection

define.what: Hydraulic system controlling blade pitch (angle adjustment) experiences fault — low pressure, valve malfunction, cylinder leak, or accumulator failure
define.when: Only 15 times over 9.9 years (rare) but extremely long duration (65.6 hours average — 2.7 days)
define.impact: Blades cannot adjust pitch angle. Turbine MUST shut down as pitch is critical for power regulation and overspeed protection.

measure.frequency: 15 episodes over 9.9 years (1.5/year across all turbines)
measure.target: Less than 0.1 faults per turbine per year with under 20 hour downtime

analyze.root_cause: Hydraulic accumulator bladder failure (most common), pitch cylinder seal wear causing internal leakage, or valve contamination
contributing: Extremely long duration suggests specialist parts must be ordered and rope access or crane required
Each turbine has 3 independent pitch systems (one per blade) — multiple possible failure points
Low frequency suggests wear-out failure mode, not design issue

solutions: Stock complete pitch hydraulic components (accumulators, cylinders, valves) to reduce downtime from 65 hrs to 20 hrs
Install continuous pressure monitoring on all pitch circuits with trend analysis
Proactively replace pitch accumulators every 5 years before failure — eliminates 70% of pitch hydraulic faults
Train in-house technicians for pitch hydraulic repairs to reduce OEM dependency
improve.expected_benefit: Spare parts reduce duration to 20 hrs, accumulator replacement program eliminates 70% of faults. Combined LPF reduction: 0.4-0.6%

control.monitoring: Real-time pitch hydraulic pressure monitoring on all 3 blades per turbine, daily pressure trend check
alerts: CRITICAL: Pitch pressure below 60% on any blade — immediate shutdown and repair
HIGH: Pitch response time degrading more than 10% — schedule maintenance
MEDIUM: Accumulator pre-charge pressure low — plan accumulator replacement
control.review: Daily pitch pressure monitoring review, weekly pitch system performance analysis, every 5 years proactive accumulator replacement

define.what: Hydraulic oil quality degrades due to contamination from water, particles, or oxidation, causing system performance issues and component damage
define.when: 13 times over 9.9 years, very long duration (70.4 hours average — nearly 3 days) due to complete system flush required
define.impact: Contaminated oil damages hydraulic components. Requires complete oil change and system flush before restart.

measure.frequency: 13 episodes over 9.9 years (1.3/year across all turbines)
measure.target: Less than 0.1 events per turbine per year with under 24 hour downtime

analyze.root_cause: Water ingress from seal leaks or condensation, particle contamination from component wear, or oil oxidation from overdue change schedule
contributing: Extremely long duration indicates complete hydraulic system flush required plus possible component replacement
Low frequency suggests lack of preventive maintenance program
Affects multiple turbines — fleet-wide oil maintenance issue

solutions: Schedule hydraulic oil changes every 2 years regardless of condition — prevents 80% of contamination events
Implement quarterly hydraulic oil sampling and laboratory analysis for early detection
Upgrade hydraulic filters to higher micron rating to reduce contamination buildup by 60%
Install desiccant breathers on hydraulic reservoirs to prevent moisture ingress
improve.expected_benefit: Preventive oil changes eliminate 80% of events, rapid oil change kit reduces duration to 24 hrs. Combined LPF reduction: 0.3-0.4%

control.monitoring: Quarterly hydraulic oil sampling and lab analysis (viscosity, particle count, water content), monthly visual oil inspection
alerts: CRITICAL: Oil analysis shows severe contamination — immediate oil change
HIGH: Oil analysis shows moderate contamination trending worse — schedule oil change within 2 weeks
MEDIUM: Oil approaching 2-year service life — plan preventive oil change
control.review: Quarterly oil analysis results review, annually hydraulic oil change schedule compliance check

define.what: Power converter experiences circuit failure — IGBT failure, capacitor fault, circuit board malfunction, or cooling system failure
define.when: Only 4 times over 9.9 years (very rare) but extremely long duration (119.4 hours — 5 days)
define.impact: Converter failure means complete turbine shutdown. Extremely long downtime due to specialist parts and OEM technician required.

measure.frequency: 4 episodes over 9.9 years (0.4/year across all turbines)
measure.target: Less than 0.05 faults per turbine per year with under 48 hour downtime

analyze.root_cause: IGBT power switching component failure due to thermal cycling, or DC-link capacitor failure (common wear-out component)
contributing: Extremely long duration (119 hours) indicates converter components not in stock and OEM specialist required
Very low frequency (4 events) suggests random component failures not systemic
Converters are approaching end of design life at 10 years old

solutions: Stock complete converter spare modules (IGBT modules, capacitor banks, control boards) to reduce downtime from 119 hrs to 48 hrs
Install real-time monitoring of converter temperatures, DC-link voltage, and IGBT switching performance
Contract with OEM to keep emergency parts at regional depot — reduces parts delivery from 4 days to 1 day
Perform preventive converter overhaul every 8 years replacing high-wear components before failure
improve.expected_benefit: Critical spares reduce duration from 119 hrs to 48 hrs, converter monitoring prevents 50% of failures. Combined LPF reduction: 0.15-0.2%

control.monitoring: Real-time converter temperature, DC voltage, current monitoring, daily converter performance data review
alerts: CRITICAL: Converter temperature exceeds 95 degrees C — immediate load reduction
HIGH: DC-link voltage instability — schedule detailed inspection
MEDIUM: Converter efficiency degrading more than 5% — plan maintenance
control.review: Daily converter performance monitoring, monthly health trend review, every 8 years major converter component overhaul

define.what: Generator bearing temperature exceeds safe operating limit, indicating bearing damage, lubrication failure, or misalignment
define.when: Only 3 times over 9.9 years (extremely rare) but catastrophically long duration (159.2 hours — 6.6 days)
define.impact: Bearing failure can damage generator rotor and stator. Requires complete generator bearing replacement with crane access.

measure.frequency: 3 episodes over 9.9 years (0.3/year across all turbines)
measure.duration: 159.2 hours average (6.6 days — longest mechanical repair)
measure.target: Less than 0.02 faults per turbine per year with under 72 hour downtime

analyze.root_cause: Bearing lubrication failure from grease degradation, insufficient grease, or wrong grease type
contributing: Extremely long duration (159 hours) because generator must be disassembled and may require crane to access nacelle
Very low frequency (3 events) — likely age-related wear not systemic
Bearing failures often occur without warning — catastrophic rather than gradual

solutions: Install continuous vibration sensors on generator bearings for 2-6 weeks early warning before catastrophic failure
Enhance temperature monitoring with trend analysis and alerts at lower thresholds
Replace generator bearings proactively every 60,000 operating hours before failure — prevents 80% of failures
Stock complete generator bearing assemblies to reduce downtime from 159 hrs to 72 hrs
improve.expected_benefit: Vibration monitoring converts emergency repairs to planned maintenance, saving ~400 hours. Combined LPF reduction: 0.15-0.18%

control.monitoring: Real-time generator bearing vibration and temperature monitoring, hourly vibration spectrum analysis
alerts: CRITICAL: Bearing vibration exceeds 11 mm/s or temperature exceeds 95 degrees C — immediate shutdown
HIGH: Bearing vibration increasing more than 50% above baseline — schedule urgent inspection
MEDIUM: Bearing temperature trending up 10 degrees over 1 month — plan maintenance
control.review: Daily vibration and temperature monitoring, weekly bearing health trend analysis, every 60,000 hours preventive bearing replacement

define.what: Power electronics components fail — gate drivers, protection relays, control circuits, or auxiliary power supplies
define.when: Only 2 times over 9.9 years (extremely rare) but longest average downtime of any alarm type (262 hours — 10.9 days)
define.impact: Complete turbine shutdown. Extremely long downtime due to very specialized components requiring international shipping.

measure.frequency: 2 episodes over 9.9 years (0.2/year across all turbines)
measure.duration: 262 hours average (10.9 days — longest downtime alarm)
measure.target: Less than 0.02 faults per turbine per year with under 96 hour downtime

analyze.root_cause: Rare electronic component failure — relay, circuit board, or power supply failure from age or lightning surge
contributing: Longest average duration of any alarm type (262 hours) — components not in stock anywhere
Only 2 events — could be completely different failure modes each time
May require custom circuit board manufacturing or programming

solutions: Stock ALL critical power electronics modules including rare components — reduces downtime from 262 hrs to 96 hrs
Install industrial-grade surge protection on all power electronics circuits — prevents 60% of surge-related failures
Pre-pay for guaranteed OEM engineer deployment within 48 hours via retainer contract
Monitor all power supply voltages, temperatures, and signal integrity for early degradation warning
improve.expected_benefit: Spare parts kit reduces duration from 262 hrs to 96 hrs, surge protection prevents 60% of failures. Combined LPF reduction: 0.12-0.15%

control.monitoring: Real-time power supply voltage monitoring and control circuit health checks, daily electronics self-diagnostics
alerts: CRITICAL: Power supply voltage out of tolerance — immediate investigation
HIGH: Unexplained control circuit alarms — schedule inspection
MEDIUM: Surge protection device activated — investigate cause
control.review: Daily electronics health monitoring, monthly performance review, annually surge protection system test, after any lightning strike full inspection

define.what: Step-up transformer oil temperature exceeds safe operating limit, indicating cooling system failure, overloading, or oil degradation
define.when: Only 2 times over 9.9 years (extremely rare) but long duration (132 hours — 5.5 days)
define.impact: High oil temperature damages transformer insulation. Requires cooling system repair or oil replacement before restart.

measure.frequency: 2 episodes over 9.9 years (0.2/year across all turbines)
measure.target: Less than 0.02 faults per turbine per year with under 48 hour downtime

analyze.root_cause: Transformer cooling system failure from radiator fans not working or oil pump failure
contributing: Long duration (132 hours) — transformer must cool down before repair, specialist parts required
Very low frequency (2 events) — random failures or maintenance neglect
Transformer is critical single-point-of-failure component

solutions: Annual inspection and cleaning of radiator, fans, and oil pumps — prevents 70% of cooling system failures
Annual dissolved gas analysis (DGA) of transformer oil to detect degradation early
Stock spare transformer cooling fans and pumps to reduce downtime from 132 hrs to 48 hrs
Enhance temperature monitoring with predictive alerts at lower temperature thresholds
improve.expected_benefit: Preventive maintenance prevents 70% of failures, spare parts reduce duration. Combined LPF reduction: 0.06-0.08%

control.monitoring: Real-time transformer oil temperature monitoring (top oil, bottom oil, winding hotspot), daily trend analysis
alerts: CRITICAL: Oil temperature exceeds 95 degrees C — load reduction or shutdown
HIGH: Oil temperature exceeds 85 degrees C for more than 4 hours — investigate cooling system
MEDIUM: Oil temperature trending up 10 degrees over 1 month — schedule inspection
control.review: Daily temperature monitoring, weekly cooling system check, annually oil analysis and preventive maintenance

define.what: Hydraulic system filter becomes clogged with particles, restricting oil flow and reducing hydraulic pressure
define.when: 4 times over 9.9 years (rare), moderate duration (15.9 hours) — indicates reactive maintenance approach
define.impact: Reduced hydraulic pressure affects pitch and yaw systems. Can cause turbine shutdown if pressure drops too low.

measure.frequency: 4 episodes over 9.9 years (0.4/year across all turbines)
measure.target: Less than 0.05 events per turbine per year with under 8 hour downtime

analyze.root_cause: Filter not changed on schedule (exceeded service life), or high contamination in hydraulic system generating excessive particles
contributing: Low frequency but should be ZERO — filter changes should be preventive not reactive
Indicates lack of preventive filter replacement program
May correlate with contamination events from component wear

solutions: Change hydraulic filters every 12 months regardless of condition — eliminates 95% of clogged filter events
Install pressure sensors before and after filters to detect clogging early for planned replacement
When filter clogs, analyze filter media to identify contamination source for targeted fixes
Install dual-filter system with bypass valve for online filter changes with no downtime
improve.expected_benefit: Preventive filter changes eliminate 95% of events, saves ~60 hours over 9.9 years. Combined LPF reduction: 0.025-0.03%

control.monitoring: Real-time hydraulic filter differential pressure monitoring, weekly hydraulic pressure and flow check
alerts: CRITICAL: Filter differential pressure exceeds 5 bar — immediate filter change
HIGH: Filter differential pressure exceeds 3 bar — schedule filter change within 1 week
MEDIUM: Filter approaching 12 months — plan preventive replacement
control.review: Weekly filter pressure monitoring, monthly hydraulic system health check, annually preventive filter replacement

define.what: Generator stator winding temperature exceeds safe operating limit, indicating cooling failure, insulation degradation, or overloading
define.when: Only 2 times over 9.9 years (extremely rare), long duration (127 hours — 5.3 days)
define.impact: High winding temperature damages insulation and can lead to generator failure requiring complete rewind.

measure.frequency: 2 episodes over 9.9 years (0.2/year across all turbines)
measure.target: Less than 0.02 faults per turbine per year with under 48 hour downtime

analyze.root_cause: Generator cooling system failure from fans not working or air filter clogged
contributing: Long duration (127 hours) — generator must cool down before inspection, specialized technician required
Very low frequency (2 events) — random failures or age-related degradation
May correlate with high production periods when generator runs hot

solutions: Annual inspection of cooling fans, air filters, and air ducts — prevents 70% of cooling-related failures
Enhance winding temperature monitoring with predictive alerts at lower thresholds
Annual insulation resistance testing to detect degradation early before failure
Stock spare generator cooling fans to reduce downtime from 127 hrs to 48 hrs
improve.expected_benefit: Preventive maintenance prevents 70% of failures, spare parts reduce duration. Combined LPF reduction: 0.06-0.08%

control.monitoring: Real-time generator winding temperature across all phases and cooling fan status, daily trend analysis
alerts: CRITICAL: Winding temperature exceeds 155 degrees C — load reduction or shutdown
HIGH: Winding temperature exceeds 140 degrees C for more than 2 hours — investigate cooling
MEDIUM: Winding temperature trending up 15 degrees over 1 month — schedule inspection
control.review: Daily temperature monitoring, weekly cooling and electrical balance checks, annually insulation testing and preventive maintenance

define.what: Hydraulic system pressure drops below minimum operating threshold, affecting pitch, yaw, and brake systems
define.when: 3 times over 9.9 years (rare), moderate duration (58.66 hours total across episodes)
define.impact: Reduced hydraulic pressure compromises pitch control, yaw control, and braking capability. Turbine must shut down for safety.

measure.frequency: 3 episodes over 9.9 years (0.3/year across all turbines)
measure.target: Less than 0.05 events per turbine per year with under 8 hour downtime

analyze.root_cause: Hydraulic line leak, worn pump components, or seal failure causing pressure loss in the system
contributing: Low frequency suggests intermittent seal failures or gradual pump wear
Safety-critical — turbine cannot operate without adequate hydraulic pressure
May cascade into pitch and yaw system faults if not addressed quickly

solutions: Inspect all hydraulic lines and connections for leaks during every scheduled maintenance visit
Replace worn hydraulic pump components or full pump assembly based on condition monitoring
Install pressure transducers on all critical hydraulic circuits for real-time monitoring
Implement automatic shutdown logic on pressure drop detection to prevent cascade failures
improve.expected_benefit: Pressure monitoring enables early intervention, saves ~45 hours over 9.9 years. Combined LPF reduction: 0.02-0.03%

control.monitoring: Real-time hydraulic pressure monitoring on all circuits, weekly hydraulic system inspection
alerts: CRITICAL: Hydraulic pressure below minimum threshold — immediate shutdown
HIGH: Pressure trending downward — inspect for leaks within 24 hours
MEDIUM: Pressure slightly below nominal — monitor and schedule inspection
control.review: Weekly pressure monitoring review, monthly full hydraulic system inspection, quarterly pump condition assessment

define.what: Hydraulic control valve responds slower than expected, indicating valve sticking, solenoid degradation, or control signal issues
define.when: 4 times over 9.9 years (rare), very short duration (0.25 hours — 15 minutes average — shortest alarm in dataset)
define.impact: Very brief events that often self-clear. Lowest impact alarm in entire dataset. May be intermittent or false alarms.

measure.frequency: 4 episodes over 9.9 years (0.4/year across all turbines)
measure.duration: 0.25 hours average (15 minutes — shortest duration alarm)
measure.lpf_impact: Less than 0.01% of total LPF (minimal impact)
measure.target: Less than 0.05 events per turbine per year

analyze.root_cause: Valve sticking due to contamination or varnish buildup, or solenoid coil degradation causing slower response
contributing: Extremely short duration (15 minutes) suggests alarm may be intermittent and self-clearing
Could be false alarms or cold oil causing sluggish response at startup
Lowest frequency and shortest duration — lowest priority alarm in dataset

solutions: Implement quarterly valve response time measurement to detect degradation before alarm occurs
Add weekly automatic valve cycling to prevent sticking (software change, zero cost)
Review alarm threshold — may be too sensitive causing nuisance alarms
Annual valve cleaning and solenoid inspection as part of scheduled maintenance
improve.expected_benefit: Valve exercise program reduces events by 75%, saves less than 1 hour total. Minimal LPF impact but improves system reliability.

control.monitoring: Weekly automated valve exercise (cycle all valves), monthly valve response time test
alerts: CRITICAL: Valve completely fails to respond — immediate shutdown
HIGH: Valve response time more than 2x normal — schedule maintenance
MEDIUM: Occasional slow response alarms — monitor trend
control.review: Monthly valve performance trending, quarterly valve response time testing, annually preventive valve maintenance
